title: Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow with Brown Spots?
excerpt: Yellowing leaves with brown spots on your Monstera can signal several common issues, from overwatering to pests. Learn how to diagnose the problem and bring your plant back to full health.
content:
Why Is My Monstera Turning Yellow with Brown Spots?

Few things are more alarming for a plant parent than watching your beloved Monstera's lush, glossy leaves start to yellow and develop unsightly brown spots. The good news? This is one of the most common Monstera problems, and once you know what to look for, it's usually very fixable. Let's walk through every possible cause and give you a clear action plan to nurse your plant back to its gorgeous, hole-punched glory.

Overwatering: The Most Common Culprit

If your Monstera is turning yellow with brown spots, overwatering is the first thing to rule out. Monsteras are tropical plants, but they don't like sitting in soggy soil. When roots stay wet for too long, they begin to rot, cutting off the plant's ability to absorb nutrients and oxygen.

Signs Your Monstera Is Overwatered

  • Yellowing leaves that feel soft or mushy
  • Brown spots that appear water-soaked or translucent at first
  • Soggy soil that takes more than 10 days to dry out
  • A musty smell coming from the pot
  • Brown, mushy roots when you check the root ball

What to Do

Allow the soil to dry out completely before watering again. Going forward, only water your Monstera when the top 2 inches of soil feel dry to the touch. Make sure your pot has pots with drainage holes and that excess water can freely escape. If you suspect root rot, unpot the plant, trim any black or mushy roots with sterile scissors, and repot into fresh, well-draining soil.

Underwatering Can Cause Brown Spots Too

Surprisingly, not enough water can also cause yellow leaves with brown spots — though the appearance is slightly different. When a Monstera is underwatered, the brown spots tend to be dry and crispy rather than soft and wet.

Signs Your Monstera Is Underwatered

  • Yellowing that starts at the leaf tips and edges
  • Dry, crispy brown spots or margins
  • Soil that is bone dry and pulling away from the pot edges
  • Leaves that look droopy or curled

The fix here is straightforward: give your plant a thorough watering, allowing water to drain completely from the bottom. Establish a consistent watering routine and check the soil every few days rather than watering on a fixed schedule.

Low Humidity and Dry Air

Monsteras are native to the rainforests of Central America, where humidity levels are high year-round. Indoor environments — especially in winter when heating systems are running — can become very dry, causing leaf stress that shows up as yellow leaves with brown, crispy edges.

Aim to keep humidity levels above 50% for your Monstera. You can boost humidity by placing a pebble tray filled with water beneath the pot, grouping plants together, or using a humidifier nearby. Misting can help temporarily, but it's not as effective as a consistent humidity source. For more tips, check out our guide on how to increase humidity for tropical houseplants.

Direct Sunlight and Sunscorch

While Monsteras love bright, indirect light, direct sunlight can scorch their leaves, causing yellow patches that quickly develop into brown, bleached-looking spots. This is especially common when plants are moved outdoors in summer or placed too close to a south-facing window.

Move your Monstera to a spot that receives bright, filtered light — ideally within a few feet of a window but protected from the sun's direct rays by a sheer curtain. Once scorched, those leaves won't recover, but protecting the plant now will prevent further damage.

Pest Infestations

Several common houseplant pests can cause yellow leaves and brown spots on Monsteras. Spider mites, scale insects, and mealybugs are among the most frequent offenders.

Common Pests to Look For

  • Spider mites: Tiny dots on the underside of leaves, often with fine webbing; cause pale, stippled yellowing
  • Scale insects: Brown, shell-like bumps on stems and leaves that suck sap and cause yellowing
  • Mealybugs: White, cottony clusters in leaf joints that weaken the plant over time
  • Thrips: Cause silvery streaks and distorted growth alongside yellowing

Inspect your plant carefully, especially the undersides of leaves and along the stems. Treat infestations with neem oil, insecticidal soap, or by wiping leaves down with a cotton ball dipped in rubbing alcohol. For a deeper dive, visit our article on identifying and treating common Monstera pests.

Nutrient Deficiencies

A lack of essential nutrients — particularly magnesium, iron, or nitrogen — can cause a condition called chlorosis, where leaves turn yellow while the veins remain green. Over time, affected areas may dry out and turn brown.

If your Monstera hasn't been fertilized in a while or is in old, depleted soil, this could be the cause. Feed your plant with a balanced, water-soluble balanced liquid fertilizer every 4–6 weeks during the growing season (spring through summer). Avoid fertilizing in fall and winter when growth naturally slows. For guidance on feeding schedules, our Monstera fertilizing guide has everything you need.

Root Bound Plants

When a Monstera outgrows its pot, its roots become compacted and can no longer efficiently uptake water and nutrients. This stress can manifest as yellowing leaves and brown spots. Check if roots are circling the bottom of the pot or poking out of drainage holes — these are signs it's time to repot.

Choose a new pot that is 2–3 inches larger in diameter than the current one, with fresh well-draining quality potting mix. Spring is the best time to repot, giving your plant the whole growing season to recover and establish.

Diagnosing the Problem: A Quick Checklist

  1. Check the soil — is it soggy, dry, or just right?
  2. Examine the leaves — are the spots soft or crispy?
  3. Look for pests on the undersides of leaves and along stems
  4. Consider recent changes — did you move the plant or change your watering schedule?
  5. Check the roots if you suspect rot or compaction
  6. Think about your last fertilizing session

Preventing Yellow Leaves in the Future

Consistency is the key to a happy Monstera. Provide bright indirect light, water only when the top inch or two of soil is dry, maintain humidity above 50%, and fertilize regularly during the growing season. Wipe down leaves monthly to remove dust and spot pests early.

With a little detective work and the right adjustments, your Monstera will be pushing out stunning new fenestrated leaves before you know it. For more help with common problems, explore our full Monstera troubleshooting series on LeafLogic.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is my Monstera turning yellow with brown spots?

The most common cause is overwatering, which leads to root rot and prevents the plant from absorbing nutrients. Other causes include underwatering, low humidity, or direct sunlight scorching the leaves.

How do I know if I am overwatering my Monstera?

Signs of overwatering include soft, mushy yellowing leaves, water-soaked brown spots, soil that stays wet for more than 10 days, and a musty smell from the pot. Check the roots — black or mushy roots confirm root rot.

Why does my Monstera have dry crispy brown spots?

Dry, crispy brown spots that start at the leaf tips and edges typically indicate underwatering or low humidity. Water your plant thoroughly and ensure humidity levels stay above 50%.

How often should I water my Monstera?

Water your Monstera only when the top 2 inches of soil feel dry to the touch, rather than on a fixed schedule. Always make sure the pot has drainage holes so excess water can escape freely.

What humidity level does a Monstera need?

Monsteras thrive at humidity levels above 50%, mimicking their native rainforest environment. You can achieve this using a humidifier, a pebble tray with water, or by grouping plants together.
